title: The geopolitics of fertiliser
gist: The supply of usable nitrogen has driven wars and technological breakthroughs from guano to saltpetre to Haber-Bosch natural gas; each transition relocated dependency rather than removing it, and the green-ammonia shift now risks new dependencies on electrolysers (China-led) and cheap renewables amid Middle East gas-price shocks.

insights.claim: The dependencies underpinning fertiliser supply have never disappeared, only relocated from guano to Chilean saltpetre to natural gas, a pattern relevant to current supply-chain resilience debates.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Core thesis: technological substitution swaps one chokepoint for another rather than achieving independence.
insights.quote: they have simply relocated from one source to the next
insights.locator: The dependencies underpinning that supply chain

insights.claim: Nitrogen scarcity has repeatedly caused war, from the 1864 Guano War over Peru's Chincha Islands to the 1879 War of the Pacific over Chilean Atacama sodium nitrate.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Historical evidence that fertiliser inputs are a recurring casus belli, framing nitrogen as strategic.
insights.quote: war broke out between Chile, Bolivia and Peru

insights.claim: The Haber-Bosch process solved saltpetre scarcity by synthesising ammonia from abundant atmospheric nitrogen, a stark example of technological substitution that revolutionised agriculture and food security.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: The canonical case of a chemical breakthrough breaking a resource chokehold.
insights.quote: revolutionised agriculture, as it secured the large-scale availability of economically feasible fertiliser

insights.claim: Whoever controls natural gas controls fertilisers and to some extent warfare, since Haber-Bosch hydrogen comes from energy-intensive steam-methane reforming of natural gas.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Links fertiliser security directly to gas markets, a transmission channel for energy shocks into food.
insights.quote: whoever controlled natural gas resources has also controlled fertilisers and, to some extent, warfare
insights.locator: Ever since, whoever controlled natural gas

insights.claim: Ammonia production is highly concentrated, with China accounting for 30% worldwide and the US, EU, India, Russia and Middle East each producing 8-10%.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Quantifies the production concentration that makes ammonia a geopolitical pressure point.
insights.quote: with Chinese production accounting for 30 per cent worldwide
insights.locator: The ammonia supply chain is highly concentrated

insights.claim: When gas prices spiked in August 2022, the EU closed 70% of its ammonia capacity and farmers faced a 149% year-on-year fertiliser price increase, raising EU import dependence on Russia.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Concrete shock magnitude showing how gas-price spikes cascade into fertiliser supply and dependency.
insights.quote: the EU industry closed 70 per cent of its ammonia capacity
insights.locator: In August 2022, when gas prices spiked

insights.claim: EU sanctions deliberately excluded Russian nitrogen fertilisers, letting Russia convert its gas surplus into fertilisers re-imported by the EU, with general European fertiliser import dependence around 45%.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Shows how food-security carve-outs preserve adversary dependency despite sanctions.
insights.quote: Russia was able to turn its natural gas surplus into nitrogen-based fertilisers that were then imported by the EU
insights.locator: The European sanctions on Russia did not include

insights.claim: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz spiked gas prices and blocked ammonia trade from major Middle Eastern exporters, with nitrogen fertiliser surges of US$100 per ton reported, threatening a supply on which roughly half the world's population depends.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: A live chokepoint event transmitting directly into global fertiliser prices and food security.
insights.quote: around half of the world's population stays alive thanks to the fertiliser industry
insights.locator: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz

insights.claim: Green ammonia (using electrolysis-based green hydrogen) could lower natural-gas dependence but currently costs 10-100% more than conventional fossil-based production.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Quantifies the cost premium gating the green-ammonia transition and its investment case.
insights.quote: between 10 and 100 per cent more expensive than the conventional production
insights.locator: At the moment, green ammonia production costs

insights.claim: A green-ammonia shift would replace gas dependency with new dependencies on electrolysers (China leads installed capacity) and regions with abundant cheap renewable energy.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Warns the energy transition rotates fertiliser dependency toward China-led electrolyser supply.
insights.quote: it might also generate new dependencies on electrolysers (China leads on installed capacity)

insights.claim: Green ammonia is positioned to become a significant energy carrier in maritime transportation, a dual-use commodity whose future fuel applications could also serve warships.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: Flags ammonia's emerging role as a shipping fuel, broadening its strategic and investment relevance.
insights.quote: green ammonia is deemed to play a significant role in the future of maritime transportation

insights.claim: Fertiliser shocks materialise over seasons rather than hours, so a situation can look merely suboptimal until structural damage has accumulated, leaving Europe now in a vulnerable interval between the old gas system breaking down and green ammonia not yet ready.
insights.speaker: Mara Balasa
insights.so_what: A timing framework: slow-moving food-input shocks are under-prioritised by policymakers until too late.
insights.quote: Technological transitions might not move countries from vulnerability to security but rather create an interval where the old system is breaking down
insights.locator: Third, the timing of transitions is crucial
